title: SEGMENT 15 — Building your own tag: HaloTag, SNAP, CLIP

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Building your own tag: HaloTag, SNAP, CLIP
There's a beautiful hybrid approach I want to spend a segment on, because it's become enormously important in modern imaging.
The idea is: instead of building the fluorescent molecule directly into your protein, build a small magnet, and then later add the fluorescent molecule of your choice.
That's the analogy.
The protein you express is a magnet — a small enzyme that doesn't glow at all.
You genetically fuse the magnet to your protein of interest, just like you'd fuse a fluorescent protein.
The magnet attaches itself to your protein wherever your protein goes.
Then, separately, you add the matching glowing payload — a small fluorescent ligand that has a chemical handle the magnet grabs onto.
The magnet snaps onto the payload covalently, and now your protein is fluorescently labeled, with the dye of your choice, at the moment of your choice.
This family of tools is called self-labeling protein tags, and the two leading systems are HaloTag and SNAP-tag.
HaloTag, developed by Promega, is an engineered version of a bacterial enzyme that normally cleaves chloride atoms off alkyl chloride substrates.
In the engineered HaloTag, a single mutation in the active site removes the step that would normally release the substrate, so the enzyme gets stuck holding it forever.
So if you provide HaloTag with a substrate molecule — a chloroalkane carrying whatever fluorescent dye you want — HaloTag will rapidly and covalently capture that dye and hold it permanently.
You express your protein fused to HaloTag, you wash on a fluorescent HaloTag ligand, and within minutes, your protein is glowing.
SNAP-tag, developed by Kai Johnsson's lab, works similarly but with different chemistry.
It's an engineered version of a human DNA-repair enzyme that normally transfers a chemical group from damaged DNA to a cysteine in its own active site.
The engineered SNAP-tag accepts ligands built on a different chemical platform, called benzylguanine.
CLIP-tag is a SNAP variant that grabs a different chemical group, benzylcytosine, so you can use both SNAP and CLIP in the same cell with two completely orthogonal labels.
So why use these instead of, or in addition to, fluorescent proteins?
Several reasons.
First, brighter dyes.
The payload you attach can be a synthetic dye — and as we just discussed, the best synthetic dyes are brighter and more photostable than any fluorescent protein.
The combination HaloTag-plus-JF-dye is, for many demanding applications like single-molecule tracking and super-resolution, dramatically better than any pure fluorescent protein.
Second, temporal control of labeling.
You can choose when to add the dye.
Express your fusion for as long as you want, wait until your cell reaches some interesting state, and only then add the label.
You can do pulse-chase experiments: label one batch of molecules today with a green dye, label a different batch tomorrow with a red dye, and watch the two populations.
Third, color flexibility.
The same expressed protein can be labeled with any color you have a ligand for.
Want green today?
Add green ligand.
Want red next week?
Add red ligand.
No re-engineering of the construct required.
Fourth, no-wash imaging for some dye chemistries.
Certain ligands are dark in solution and only become fluorescent when bound to the tag — so you can add them and image immediately, no washing required, with very low background.
Fifth, the chemistry can deliver things other than fluorophores.
You can attach biotin for affinity purification, photocrosslinkers for capturing interactions, drug molecules for chemical perturbations.
The tag becomes a universal hook.
The trade-offs: self-labeling tags require an added ligand, and the ligand has to get into the cell or tissue, which can be a constraint in vivo.
They're a bit bigger than the smallest peptide-based labels but smaller than tandem-dimer fluorescent proteins.
The way to think about HaloTag and SNAP-tag is not as competitors to fluorescent proteins but as complementary tools occupying different parts of the toolbox.
For an experiment where you want pure genetic encoding with no added reagent — a fluorescent protein is right.
For an experiment where you need maximum brightness, temporal control, or color flexibility — self-labeling tags shine.
Many modern experiments use both: a fluorescent protein for one channel, a HaloTag plus synthetic dye for another.
The flexible-but-needs-a-reagent approach and the all-in-one-genetic approach are both alive and well, and the best experiments draw on both.
